text: Endocrinology follow-up visit. The patient's type 2 diabetes mellitus remains suboptimally controlled, with a hemoglobin A1c of 8.2% today, up from 7.6% three months ago. Home glucose logs show fasting readings between 140 and 180 mg/dL and frequent post-prandial spikes above 220 mg/dL. Metformin was increased to 1000 mg twice daily and we discussed adding a GLP-1 receptor agonist at the next visit if the A1c has not improved. Reviewed hypoglycemia warning signs, sick-day rules, and the importance of consistent carbohydrate intake at meals. Continuous glucose monitoring was offered and the patient will consider it. Annual diabetic retinopathy screening and foot examination were scheduled. Repeat A1c and basic metabolic panel in three months.
notes: diabetes topic A; pairs with doc-diabetes-education for the multi-relevant query rq-001

text: Diabetes self-management education session. The patient attended a one-hour session with the certified diabetes educator focused on dietary management of blood glucose. Topics covered: carbohydrate counting with a target of 45-60 grams per meal, reading nutrition labels, the plate method for portion control, and timing of meals relative to metformin dosing. The patient demonstrated correct use of the home glucometer and recorded a return demonstration of checking fasting blood sugar. Discussed how exercise lowers blood glucose and agreed on a goal of walking 30 minutes five days per week. Follow-up education session scheduled in six weeks to review the glucose log and reinforce carbohydrate counting skills.
notes: diabetes topic B; pairs with doc-diabetes-mgmt for the multi-relevant query rq-001

text: Hospital admission note, cardiology service. The patient presented to the emergency department with three days of progressively worsening dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea requiring three pillows, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. He reports a 4 kg weight gain over the past week and new bilateral lower-extremity edema extending to the mid-shin. He admits to dietary indiscretion over the holiday, including salted cured meats, and ran out of his loop diuretic five days ago. On examination he is tachypneic at rest with a respiratory rate of 24, oxygen saturation 89% on room air improving to 94% on 2 liters nasal cannula. Jugular venous pressure is elevated at 12 cm. Auscultation reveals bibasilar crackles halfway up both lung fields and an S3 gallop. There is 2+ pitting edema bilaterally. Chest radiograph shows pulmonary vascular congestion, cephalization, and small bilateral pleural effusions. BNP is markedly elevated at 1450 pg/mL, up from a baseline of 300. Troponin is negative on two serial draws, making acute coronary syndrome unlikely. The clinical picture is consistent with acute decompensated heart failure with volume overload, precipitated by medication non-adherence and dietary sodium excess. Management plan: admit to telemetry, intravenous furosemide 80 mg twice daily with strict intake and output documentation, daily standing weights before breakfast, fluid restriction to 1.5 liters per day, and a 2 gram sodium diet. The home guideline-directed medical therapy will be continued, with the beta blocker held only if signs of cardiogenic shock develop, which are not present now. An updated transthoracic echocardiogram has been ordered to reassess ejection fraction, last measured at 35% one year ago. Electrolytes and renal function will be checked twice daily during active diuresis, with potassium repletion per protocol to maintain a level above 4.0. Case management has been consulted to arrange a home scale, medication delivery, and a follow-up appointment in the heart failure clinic within seven days of discharge, given that this is his second admission for volume overload this year. Patient education on daily weight monitoring, the 2 gram sodium diet, and a diuretic action plan was started on day one and will be reinforced by nursing before discharge.
notes: deliberately >2000 chars so it splits into 2+ chunks and exercises document-level dedup; single relevant target for rq-002

text: Pediatric asthma action plan review. The child was seen with her mother to update the written asthma action plan for the new school year. Green zone: no cough, wheeze, or nighttime waking; continue the daily inhaled corticosteroid controller, fluticasone one puff twice daily with the spacer. Yellow zone: cough, wheezing, or chest tightness with play; give albuterol two puffs via spacer every four hours as needed and continue the controller; call the clinic if the rescue inhaler is needed more than twice per week. Red zone: hard or fast breathing, retractions, trouble speaking in sentences, or albuterol needed more often than every four hours; give albuterol immediately and seek emergency care. Inhaler and spacer technique were observed and corrected. A copy of the plan was provided for the school nurse along with a signed albuterol authorization form.

Orthopedic post-operative visit, two weeks after right total knee arthroplasty. The surgical incision is clean, dry, and intact with staples removed today and steri-strips applied. Range of motion is currently 5 to 95 degrees; the goal is full extension and at least 110 degrees of flexion by six weeks. The patient is ambulating with a front-wheeled walker and may advance to a cane as tolerated. Physical therapy will continue three times weekly, emphasizing quadriceps strengthening, heel slides, and gradual progression on the stationary bicycle. Ice and elevation were recommended for post-exercise swelling. Deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis with aspirin continues for four more weeks. Driving may resume once off opioid pain medication and able to brake safely, typically around four to six weeks for a right knee. Next follow-up with radiographs in four weeks.

Primary care follow-up for hypertension. Home blood pressure log shows an average of 152/94 over the past two weeks, despite reported adherence to lisinopril 20 mg daily. Office reading today is 156/96, confirmed on repeat after five minutes of rest. The patient drinks two to three cups of coffee daily and admits to frequent takeout meals high in sodium. No headache, chest pain, or visual changes. Examination is otherwise unremarkable and there is no lower-extremity edema. Plan: add amlodipine 5 mg daily to the existing lisinopril, counsel on the DASH diet and reducing sodium to under 2 grams per day, and continue home blood pressure monitoring twice daily. Basic metabolic panel ordered to check potassium and creatinine after the medication change. Return visit in four weeks; if blood pressure remains above 140/90, will consider increasing amlodipine to 10 mg.
notes: hypertension topic A; pairs with doc-htn-med-review for the multi-relevant query rq-003

text: Pharmacist-led antihypertensive medication review. The patient's current regimen is lisinopril 20 mg every morning and amlodipine 5 mg every evening. He reports occasional ankle swelling since starting amlodipine, a known side effect of dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers, currently mild and not bothersome. No dry cough on the ACE inhibitor. Potassium 4.1 and creatinine stable on last month's labs. Adherence check found he misses roughly two doses per month, usually when traveling; a seven-day pill organizer and a phone reminder were set up. Discussed taking blood pressure readings seated, feet flat, after five minutes of rest, and bringing the home cuff in for calibration against the office device. If ankle edema worsens, options include splitting the amlodipine dose or switching to a thiazide. Follow-up review in three months.
notes: hypertension topic B; pairs with doc-hypertension-visit for the multi-relevant query rq-003

text: Discharge summary following a five-day admission for acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. The patient presented with increased dyspnea, a productive cough with purulent sputum, and increased rescue inhaler use over four days, on a background of severe COPD with a FEV1 of 42% predicted and a 40 pack-year smoking history, quit two years ago. He was treated with nebulized albuterol and ipratropium every four hours, a five-day course of oral prednisone 40 mg daily completed in hospital, and azithromycin for five days, the final two doses to be taken at home. He weaned from 4 liters of supplemental oxygen back to his baseline of 2 liters via nasal cannula, and ambulatory oximetry before discharge showed saturations of 90 to 92 percent on that baseline flow. Discharge medications: tiotropium dry powder inhaler once daily, budesonide-formoterol two puffs twice daily, albuterol metered dose inhaler as needed with spacer, and home oxygen at 2 liters continuous. Inhaler technique was verified by respiratory therapy on the day of discharge, and the patient demonstrated correct use of the dry powder device after one correction. Written instructions were provided on recognizing early warning signs of another flare: increasing breathlessness, a change in sputum color or volume, ankle swelling, or needing the rescue inhaler more than every four hours. He was instructed to keep the emergency action plan on the refrigerator and to call the pulmonology nurse line at the first sign of worsening rather than waiting. Pulmonary rehabilitation referral was placed, with enrollment expected within three weeks, and he was strongly encouraged to attend given the demonstrated mortality and readmission benefit. Influenza and updated pneumococcal vaccination were administered prior to discharge. Follow-up appointments: primary care in one week for a medication reconciliation and pulmonology in four weeks with repeat spirometry. He was counseled that continued smoking abstinence is the single most important factor in slowing disease progression, and his two-year quit anniversary was acknowledged and reinforced.
notes: deliberately >2000 chars so it splits into 2+ chunks; single relevant target for rq-006

text: Neurology consultation for chronic migraine. The patient describes throbbing unilateral headaches occurring 10 to 12 days per month, lasting 6 to 24 hours, accompanied by photophobia, phonophobia, and nausea. About a third are preceded by a visual aura of shimmering zigzag lines lasting 20 minutes. Current abortive therapy is sumatriptan 100 mg, effective when taken early, used about 9 days per month, raising concern for medication overuse headache. Neurological examination is normal and prior brain MRI was unremarkable. Plan: start a preventive agent, topiramate titrated from 25 mg nightly up to 50 mg twice daily over four weeks, with counseling about paresthesias, word-finding difficulty, and the need for adequate hydration. Limit triptan use to no more than 8 days per month. A headache diary was provided to track frequency, triggers, and response. If topiramate is not tolerated or ineffective after three months, a CGRP monoclonal antibody will be discussed.

Routine prenatal visit at 28 weeks gestation. The patient reports good fetal movement and no contractions, leaking of fluid, or vaginal bleeding. Blood pressure 112/70, weight gain on track at 8 kg total, urine dip negative for protein and glucose. Fundal height measures 28 cm, consistent with dates. Fetal heart tones by Doppler are 148 beats per minute. The one-hour glucose challenge performed last week was normal at 118 mg/dL. She received the Tdap vaccine today per the third-trimester schedule, and Rh immune globulin was administered given her Rh-negative status. Discussed the normal pattern of fetal kick counts, warning signs including severe headache and decreased fetal movement, and the plan to begin biweekly visits from 30 weeks. Anemia screen showed hemoglobin of 10.8; started an iron supplement with vitamin C and dietary counseling on iron-rich foods.

Psychiatry medication management visit for major depressive disorder. The patient reports partial improvement after eight weeks on sertraline 100 mg daily: mood is brighter in the mornings and she has resumed cooking, but persistent low energy, poor concentration at work, and early-morning awakening remain. PHQ-9 today is 12, down from 19 at intake. No suicidal ideation, no manic symptoms, tolerating the medication without gastrointestinal upset or sexual side effects. Given the partial response and good tolerability, sertraline was increased to 150 mg daily. Sleep hygiene reviewed, with a plan to keep a consistent wake time and limit screens after 10 pm. She continues weekly cognitive behavioral therapy and finds the behavioral activation homework helpful. Safety plan reviewed and unchanged. Follow-up in four weeks with a repeat PHQ-9; if response remains partial at the higher dose, augmentation options will be discussed.

Nephrology follow-up for stage 3b chronic kidney disease. Estimated GFR is 38 mL/min, down from 42 six months ago; urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio is 420 mg/g. Blood pressure at goal on losartan, which is continued for its renoprotective effect; potassium acceptable at 4.8. The patient was counseled to avoid nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs entirely, to hold metformin and the SGLT2 inhibitor during any dehydrating illness, and to limit dietary protein to about 0.8 g/kg/day with a renal dietitian referral placed. Phosphorus and parathyroid hormone were checked given the falling GFR; vitamin D was low and repletion started. Discussed the expected trajectory, the eGFR threshold at which fistula planning and transplant evaluation conversations would begin, and reinforced that blood pressure and glycemic control are the strongest modifiable factors in slowing progression. Labs and follow-up in three months.

Cardiology visit for newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation. The patient presented with two weeks of palpitations and reduced exercise tolerance; an ECG confirmed atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 112. He remains in atrial fibrillation today but is hemodynamically stable and asymptomatic at rest. CHA2DS2-VASc score is 4, indicating a clear indication for anticoagulation, and apixaban 5 mg twice daily was started today after reviewing renal function. We discussed that the blood thinner reduces stroke risk substantially and that missed doses leave him unprotected given its short half-life. Rate control was initiated with metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, targeting a resting heart rate under 110. A transthoracic echocardiogram is ordered to assess left atrial size and ventricular function, and thyroid studies were sent. The rhythm-versus-rate control decision, including catheter ablation, will be revisited once imaging returns. Counseled on limiting alcohol, and to seek urgent care for chest pain, syncope, or signs of bleeding.

Emergency department visit for right lower leg cellulitis. The patient noticed redness and warmth spreading over the shin for two days after a minor scrape while gardening, now with tenderness and mild swelling. Temperature 38.1, otherwise vital signs normal. Examination shows a well-demarcated area of erythema and warmth over the anterior right shin, tender to palpation, without fluctuance, crepitus, or purulent drainage; no red streaking toward the knee. The border was outlined with a skin marker to track progression. Point-of-care ultrasound showed no drainable abscess and no evidence of deep vein thrombosis. Diagnosis: uncomplicated non-purulent cellulitis. Treatment: cephalexin 500 mg four times daily for seven days, elevation of the leg, and ibuprofen for discomfort. Strict return precautions were given for spreading redness beyond the marked border, worsening pain, fever above 38.5, or red streaks, and a wound check with the primary care clinic was arranged within 48 hours.

Gastroenterology clinic note for gastroesophageal reflux disease. The patient reports retrosternal burning three to four evenings per week, worse after late dinners and red wine, with occasional sour regurgitation when lying flat. No dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, or melena, and no anemia on recent labs, so no alarm features are present. He has been using calcium carbonate tablets with only brief relief. Plan: start omeprazole 20 mg once daily taken 30 to 60 minutes before breakfast for an eight-week trial, elevate the head of the bed, avoid meals within three hours of lying down, and reduce evening alcohol. If symptoms persist on the proton pump inhibitor, we will discuss upper endoscopy to evaluate for erosive esophagitis or Barrett esophagus, particularly given his ten-year symptom history. A structured step-down plan was outlined so the medication is not continued indefinitely without reassessment.
notes: distractor topic; not targeted by any query

text: Endocrinology visit for postmenopausal osteoporosis. DEXA scan shows a T-score of -2.8 at the lumbar spine and -2.5 at the femoral neck, meeting the densitometric definition of osteoporosis; FRAX ten-year major fracture risk is 21%. There is no history of fragility fracture, no height loss on stadiometer measurement, and vertebral fracture assessment was negative. Secondary causes were excluded: normal calcium, vitamin D now replete at 38 ng/mL after supplementation, normal thyroid studies and celiac screen. Alendronate 70 mg once weekly was started, with careful instruction to take it fasting with a full glass of water and remain upright for 30 minutes. Calcium intake of 1200 mg daily, preferably dietary, and vitamin D 2000 units daily were advised, along with twice-weekly resistance exercise and a home fall-safety review. Repeat DEXA in two years; drug holiday will be considered after five years of therapy.
notes: distractor topic; not targeted by any query
